narrative: Dear [Student's Name], I know this rejection letter stings, especially after all the effort you've poured into your journey toward law school, and I want to walk you through why our predictive model saw your path to the bar exam as riskier than we can take on right now—it foresaw a 63% chance of not passing, based on patterns from thousands of students like you. At the heart of it was your Year 3 decile of 2, which stood out as the biggest signal, whispering to the model that even with your earlier potential, your performance dipped into tougher territory by the end of undergrad. Layered on top was your LSAT score of 32, which, while solid for many, pulled strongly in the direction of past students who struggled to bridge that gap to bar success. Your Year 1 decile of 1 added to that early cautionary tale, suggesting a foundation that hadn't quite solidified as hoped. Coming from a middle-income family painted a picture of fewer resources to lean on during those grueling study marathons, nudging the outlook lower still. Your undergraduate GPA of 3.4, though respectable, fit into a pattern where it wasn't the anchor needed to offset the rest. As a Black woman pursuing full-time studies, these aspects together echoed stories of others who've faced steeper climbs, each one tipping the scales toward the model's worry about failure. It's not about your dreams or determination—they shine through—but about how this specific combination has tripped up so many before, and we have to be honest about those odds. You've got real strengths here, like that full-time commitment, and with targeted boosts in areas like test prep or GPA-building strategies, you could rewrite this story. We're rooting for you to find the right path forward—keep pushing, because your potential is there beneath it all.
